metadata.summary: Configure HorizontalPodAutoscaler (v2), PodDisruptionBudget, and KEDA for Kubernetes workload scaling and high-availability guarantees during cluster maintenance and node drains.

content.title: Kubernetes Scaling and Availability: HPA and PDB
content.summary: HorizontalPodAutoscaler (autoscaling/v2) automatically scales Deployment replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. PodDisruptionBudget protects a minimum number of running pods during voluntary disruptions such as node drains or cluster upgrades. Both are required for production deployments: HPA handles load changes, PDB handles maintenance.

why.p: Without HPA, a traffic spike causes degraded response times and potential pod OOM-kills until a human manually increases replicas. Without PDB, a cluster upgrade or node drain can evict all pods of a Deployment simultaneously — even if minAvailable in the HPA is set correctly, the Kubernetes eviction API does not check replica count by default. PDB is the only mechanism that constrains voluntary disruption.

list.item: Adding autoscaling to a production Deployment or StatefulSet.
Protecting a service during cluster maintenance, node drain, or voluntary disruption.
Tuning scale-down conservatism to prevent flapping.
Setting up event-driven autoscaling with KEDA (queue depth, HTTP request count).
Development/staging single-replica Deployments — HPA complexity without HA benefit.
Batch jobs and CronJobs — use KEDA or manual scaling on completion events instead.
StatefulSets with ordered provisioning where rapid scale-up risks data consistency — review before applying aggressive scale policies.

section.name: hpa-configuration
section.p: HPA autoscaling/v2 supports multiple simultaneous metrics (CPU, memory, custom, external). The controller polls every 15 seconds (default). Scale-up is aggressive by default (stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0). Scale-down is conservative (stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300) to prevent flapping after a traffic spike.

rule.statement: Configure HPA with minReplicas >= 2 for HA. Set scaleDown.stabilizationWindowSeconds to at least 300 seconds and limit scale-down to max 10% per 60 seconds or max 2 pods per 60 seconds (whichever is smaller). Set scaleUp.stabilizationWindowSeconds to 0 to respond immediately to spikes.
rule.rationale: Aggressive scale-down after a traffic spike will cause a second spike when load returns. The conservative scale-down window prevents thrashing. Immediate scale-up ensures capacity is added before latency degrades.

example.title: HPA v2 with CPU, memory, and advanced behavior
example.code:
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: api-hpa spec: scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: api minReplicas: 3 maxReplicas: 20 metrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 70 - type: Resource resource: name: memory target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 80
# Advanced behavior (2025-2026) behavior: scaleDown: stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300 # Wait 5 min before scale down policies: - type: Percent value: 10 # Max 10% reduction periodSeconds: 60 - type: Pods value: 2 # Max 2 pods periodSeconds: 60 selectPolicy: Min # Use most conservative
scaleUp: stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 # Scale up immediately policies: - type: Percent value: 100 # Double pods if needed periodSeconds: 15 - type: Pods value: 4 # Or add 4 pods periodSeconds: 15 selectPolicy: Max # Use most aggressive

list.item: CPU — general compute workloads. Target 70% utilization.
Custom — request rate, queue depth, application-specific. Use Prometheus Adapter.
External — cloud metrics (Datadog, CloudWatch). Requires external metrics API adapter.

checklist.check: maxReplicas set with cluster capacity in mind.
Metrics server running in cluster (required for CPU/memory HPA).

section.name: pdb-configuration
section.p: PodDisruptionBudget (policy/v1) defines how many pods of a selected set can be voluntarily disrupted simultaneously. Use minAvailable (minimum pods that must stay up) OR maxUnavailable (maximum pods that can be down) — not both. The Kubernetes eviction API respects PDB before draining a node; if the PDB would be violated, the drain blocks until it can proceed safely.

rule.statement: Create a PodDisruptionBudget for every production Deployment and StatefulSet with replicas >= 2. Set minAvailable to at least 1 (or N-1 for critical services). Never set minAvailable equal to replicas — that makes the PDB impossible to satisfy during a rolling update.
rule.rationale: Without a PDB, kubectl drain evicts all pods of a Deployment from a node simultaneously, causing a complete outage. PDB also ensures that rolling updates can complete — if minAvailable: replicas is set, the update will deadlock because it cannot remove the old pod without violating the budget.

example.title: PDB for production API
example.code: apiVersion: policy/v1 kind: PodDisruptionBudget metadata: name: api-pdb spec: minAvailable: 2 # At least 2 pods must remain selector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: api
example.note: With 3 replicas and minAvailable: 2, only 1 pod can be disrupted at a time. Node drain will proceed one pod at a time, waiting for each evicted pod to be rescheduled before evicting the next.

list.item: 5+ replicas: minAvailable N-1 or 80%, maxUnavailable 1 or 20% — large deployments.

checklist.check: PDB created for every production Deployment/StatefulSet.
Value allows for rolling updates (minAvailable is less than replicas).
PDB verified with kubectl get pdb after creation.

section.name: supporting-resources
section.p: Full HPA and PDB templates for production use:

example.title: PDB template
example.code: # pdb.yaml apiVersion: policy/v1 kind: PodDisruptionBudget metadata: name: ${APP_NAME}-pdb namespace: ${NAMESPACE} spec: minAvailable: 2 selector: matchLabels: app.kubernetes.io/name: ${APP_NAME}

example.title: HPA template
example.code: # hpa.yaml apiVersion: autoscaling/v2 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: ${APP_NAME}-hpa namespace: ${NAMESPACE} spec: scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: ${APP_NAME} minReplicas: ${MIN_REPLICAS:-3} maxReplicas: ${MAX_REPLICAS:-20} metrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 70 - type: Resource resource: name: memory target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 80 behavior: scaleDown: stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300 policies: - type: Percent value: 10 periodSeconds: 60 scaleUp: stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0 policies: - type: Percent value: 100 periodSeconds: 15 - type: Pods value: 4 periodSeconds: 15 selectPolicy: Max
